serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of roasted sunflower seeds is approximately 30g (one small handful), providing around 175 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Sunflower seed kernels, dry roasted, without salt added
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for dry-roasted sunflower seed kernels without salt. Core macros cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo database (F09030016). HPB values used for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron as primary canonical source. USDA used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and minerals not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for roasted unsalted sunflower seeds in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on published values for mixed seeds and nuts (GI range 15–40) from Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008. GL per serving calculated as: (35 × 24.07 × 0.30) / 100 = 2.5, rounded to 3.0 for a 30g serving. The low GI is consistent with the food's high fat, protein, and fibre content which attenuates glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g) and high potassium (~645 mg/100g) with 11.1g dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Roasted unsalted sunflower seeds are highly suitable for hypertension management due to negligible sodium (3 mg/100g) and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio; limit portions to ~30g per serving to avoid excess caloric intake that may contribute to weight-related blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), high dietary fibre (11.1g/100g), high protein (19.3g/100g), and low sugar (2.73g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted unsalted sunflower seeds have a low glycaemic index (~35) and high fibre and protein content that attenuate postprandial glucose response; a 30g portion is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes, and honey-roasted or flavoured variants should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High polyunsaturated fat (32.7g/100g, predominantly linoleic acid), high vitamin E (26.1mg/100g), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted unsalted sunflower seeds provide a cardioprotective lipid profile dominated by polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin E, with zero trans fat and cholesterol; a daily portion of ~30g can support LDL reduction and endothelial health as part of a heart-healthy diet, though total caloric density should be monitored.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in vitamin E, selenium, magnesium, and linoleic acid with zero trans fat supporting anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted unsalted sunflower seeds qualify for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out and are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; limit to ~30g daily and pair with omega-3 sources such as walnuts or flaxseed for broader anti-inflammatory coverage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted unsalted sunflower seeds (Helianthus annuus) are the dried, heat-roasted kernels of the sunflower plant, consumed without any added salt. They are minimally processed through dry-roasting, which enhances their nutty flavour and crisp texture while preserving most of their nutritional profile. The seeds are small, flat, and oval-shaped with a mild, rich, slightly buttery taste. Nutritionally, they are energy-dense at 582 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content (49.8g/100g) dominated by polyunsaturated fatty acids (32.7g/100g, primarily linoleic acid) and a meaningful monounsaturated fat fraction (9.5g/100g). Protein content is substantial at 19.3g/100g, making them a valuable plant-based protein source. Dietary fibre is high at 11.1g/100g, supporting satiety and glycaemic control. Sodium is negligible at 3mg/100g, making them ideal for low-sodium dietary patterns. They are an outstanding source of vitamin E (26.1mg/100g), selenium (79.3mcg/100g), magnesium (129mg/100g), phosphorus (660mg/100g), and folate (237mcg DFE/100g). Their low glycaemic index (~35) and high fibre and protein content make them suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. They are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Commonly consumed as a snack, salad topping, or ingredient in trail mixes, granola, and baked goods.
common_user_queries: Are sunflower seeds good for diabetes?
High protein seeds for plant-based diet

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (F09030016) with 0.95 confidence. Supplementary micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170563) for dry-roasted unsalted sunflower seed kernels — an exact match. Both approved primary sources agree on the food identity and preparation method.
notes: Saturated fat value of 5.2g/100g, monounsaturated fat of 9.5g/100g, polyunsaturated fat of 32.7g/100g, trans fat of 0.0g/100g, potassium of 645mg/100g, cholesterol of 0.0mg/100g, and all vitamin and mineral values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170563). Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). Vitamin D is 0.0 (naturally zero — seeds are not a dietary vitamin D source). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI entry for roasted unsalted sunflower seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. The food is classified under 'Meat and alternatives / Nuts and seeds' per Singapore HPB taxonomy. No allergen declarations apply to the plain unsalted seed; however, cross-contamination with tree nuts is possible in commercial settings. The MUFA/PUFA carve-out for rheumatoid arthritis is applied based on the food's dominant oleic and linoleic acid profile and high vitamin E content.
